entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
This course includes the following topic(s): Grammar: Grade 6, Dictation: Grade 6, Penmanship & Copywork: Grade 6, Written Narration: Grade 6

About Grammar: Grade 6
Students deepen their understanding of the parts of speech.

About Dictation: Grade 6
Students use selected passages to practice dictation skills. (Alveary Copywork & Dictation Books provide selected passages).

About Penmanship & Copywork: Grade 6
Students copy short passages from their books. (Alveary Copywork & Dictation Books provide selected passages).

About Written Narration: Grade 6
Students continue to gain fluency in writing through written narrations.

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content:
Grammar: Grade 6
If your student did not do Sentence Island the previous year, you should incorporate that book with the 6th-grade grammar book.

Written Narration: Grade 6
Increase the number of written narrations at a pace at which your student can be successful.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable

Grammar: Grade 6
☐ Bookmark Grammar Town Teacher Manual with select pages of answer key (Find in Quick Links)

☐ Bookmark Practice Town Teacher Manual answer key (Find in Quick Links)

Penmanship & Copywork: Grade 6
If you are using the Getty-Dubay Italic Handwriting Series, complete half a page per day or go at student's pace.

Written Narration: Grade 6
☐ Plan to complete five written narrations every week and consider increasing to six by the end of the year. 

☐ Written narrations can happen after reading at the end of any lesson, if time allows, and do not need to take up an extra 10 minutes in your schedule. This tracker serves to help you keep records of what was done throughout the year.

☐ To keep written narrations fresh and interesting, have your student try narrating in different ways (specific suggestions are sometimes included directly in a lesson): In addition to just “what did we read about?”, you can ask students to:
- “Write the story of ___.” (Example: “Write the story of the battle of Troy.”) 
- Imagine they are a character in the story and write what happened as a diary entry (Example: pretend you are Christian in Pilgrim’s Progress and write a letter or diary entry telling what happened to him in today’s chapter.”
- Pretend they are the author of the book and have them write their narration as if they are telling the story of the chapter they read for the book's first readers. (Then have them read it to someone who hasn't read the book!)
- For those that want a challenge, tell back the reading as a poem, a scene in a play, or a comic strip style narration combining illustration and words.

☐ Make sure students are practicing typing skills regularly, and have them increasingly type more narrations as they become comfortable.

☐ Encourage students to spend a full ten minutes or so on their narrations and give as much detail as possible. It may help if you encourage them to think of writing their narrations for a friend or someone who has never read the book rather than for you, who might already know the story. If they feel like they've run out of things to write, have them try one of the narration variations above.

☐ Remind students that narration is a way for them to process and think more deeply about what they read. Narration isn't about proving to you that they read a book or did an assignment but a chance for them to actually know it and better remember it themselves.

entries.title: Term Prep & Teacher Tips
entries.content:
Written Narration: Grade 6
Some ideas on supporting students who are building fluency with written narrations: 
- Write down words on a whiteboard if a student wants to use a word they don’t know how to spell.
- Have students write down brief sentences (even numbered statements) telling the sequence of events, and then have them orally give more details about each of them.
- Write the beginning and then orally tell the rest, or vice versa.
- Some students may find writing easier if you provide external supports before writing, such as index cards that can be arranged and re-ordered. You can also encourage students to talk aloud as they write or use speech-to-text and then edit.

links.label: Grammar Town Exam Questions Answer Key for Terms 1-3

blocks.text: Select a passage that has already been read and narrated. (We provide passages in the Dictation packets.) Have students read over it and select 4-5 words that they don't know. They can also mark punctuation that may be difficult to remember. They only need to select a section that contains 4-5 new words. If this is only a couple of sentences, that is fine.

Ask students which words they think will be most difficult to spell and why. If there is a common spelling pattern, you can point that out. You can also point out other words that have a similar pattern.
Have students visualize the words by "seeing" them with their eyes closed. They should also practice on a whiteboard.
As part of the same lesson, when students feel ready, slowly call out a sentence from what they studied. You will want to read it phrase by phrase and not repeat it.
When finished, have students look over their own work to check and see that everything looks right. If you see any misspelled words, erase those and have the students practice those again.
Link back to the previous lesson.

Read the assigned pages and discuss new ideas. It is important for students to be able to talk about the concepts in their own words. The pictures and diagrams in the book can serve as a springboard for discussions about the grammar concepts.
Complete practice examples in the text and/or the practice book that accompany the lesson.
Observe examples of concepts in books being read and in daily life.

lessons.body:
Tracker: ⬚ ⬚ ⬚ ⬚ ⬚ 
⍞ Materials: Grade 6 Student packet of copywork & dictation passages

➜ COPYWORK 
Spend ten minutes daily working on copywork this week. You can use this week's suggested passage or choose your own of a similar length. Spend the first 3-5 minutes of your copywork lesson focusing on penmanship by practicing a few letters in print or cursive from the Penny Gardner book or another style of your choice.

lessons.title: 15m Dictation: Grade 6 - Tracker
lessons.body:
Tracker: ⬚ ⬚ ⬚
⍞ Materials: Grade 6 Student packet of copywork & dictation passages

➜ DICTATION 
Three Dictation lessons are scheduled each week. In each lesson, there should be time for studying (10 minutes) and time for the sentences to be dictated (5 min). You can use this week's suggested passage for the lessons and/or choose your own generally from the history, literature, or geography books the student has already read during the week. The suggested passage might take one lesson to learn, or it might take all three lessons to learn. It will be different for each student. Be sure to pay attention to any notes regarding grammar or punctuation added to the student dictation packet for the week.

The student should study enough text to include 4-5 unknown words. This might mean that the student is only studying a couple of sentences. Ask for the words they do not know how to spell. Write them down on a chalkboard, whiteboard, or piece of paper. The student should work on visualizing each word until they can see it with their eyes shut, and then practice writing the word, drawing the letters in the air, or saying the letters aloud until it is memorized.

Discuss any interesting spelling patterns or punctuation from the text being studied. The process should take around 10 minutes to this point.

Then, choose a sentence or two to slowly dictate a few words at a time to the student from the text studied. Read it once. Correct any errors and revisit any words missed.  

Be sure to adapt the amount studied and dictated to suit the student. Add to or subtract from the amount or difficulty of the text studied to ensure the student's success and growth.

lessons.title: 10m Written Narration: Grade 6 - Tracker
lessons.body:
Tracker: ⬚ ⬚ ⬚ ⬚ ⬚

➜ WRITTEN NARRATION 
Complete five written narrations this week. Make sure to write from a variety of different subjects either over the course of the term or at least the year.

➜ NOTE 
Written narrations can happen after reading at the end of any lesson if time allows and do not need to take up an extra 10 minutes in your schedule. This tracker serves to help you keep records of what was done throughout the year.

If the student is new to written narration, they may fatigue quickly. Allow them to initially write a bit and tell the rest. Some may do better by first orally narrating and discussing as usual, then writing a sentence or so of that narration. Others may do better by beginning their narration in writing, then completing orally. The amount that is written can be extended over time. If students are typing fluently, consider allowing them to type their written narrations.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
See the lesson plan intro pages for ideas on troubleshooting and ideas on how to keep written narrations fresh and interesting!

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 6 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Introduction  
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town

➜ RECAP
What do you remember about grammar and sentences? Can you recite
the eight parts of speech?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.2-6

➜ PRACTICE
• Copy the list of prefixes on p.6 in your Dictation & Grammar notebook. 
• Put a check beside the ones you know already. Study the rest. 
• How many words can you find in them? For example, pro-ject or pre-dict
lessons.teacherNotes:
These are the four sections of
the orchestra in the symphony
that make a sentence. They play
in unison and in harmony.

Kinds of Words  
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.8-11 

➜ PRACTICE
Copy the diagram on p.11. Explain why it is constructed the way it is, based on what you know about the eight parts of speech.
lessons.teacherNotes: The parts of speech provide instructions for vocabulary usage. Notice how dictionaries are organized.

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 6 - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
Nouns
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.12-13 

➜ PRACTICE
List three proper nouns, three common nouns, three singular nouns, and three plural nouns.

➜ DISCUSS
Nouns can also name ideas, like "democracy" or "sympathy." Can you think of other nouns that are ideas?

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 6 - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
Pronouns
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.14-17

➜ PRACTICE
Copy the sentence at the bottom of p.16 and circle all the pronouns. Then sort them into two lists: Subject Pronouns and Object Pronouns.

➜ DISCUSS
If you are studying Latin, see if you can figure out the equivalent of subject nouns/pronouns and object nouns/pronouns. [nominative/accusative]

➜ NOTE	
If a possessive pronoun is modifying a noun, such as his book, then his is an adjective. My, your, his, her, its, our, and their are possessive adjectives.
lessons.teacherNotes: In modern usage, they and them have been adopted as singular pronouns that can replace a singular noun. For example, in the dictation lesson, the student will choose words that are unfamiliar to "them" (instead of saying "her or him").

lessons.body:
Pronouns
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.18-19

➜ PRACTICE
Reread "Heliocentrism." Try to figure out why each pronoun says what it does.

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 6 - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
Adjectives
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.20-23

➜ PRACTICE
Find all the adjectives in the list on p.22. [early, gray, slow, cool, tall] Why are the others NOT adjectives?
lessons.teacherNotes: Do NOT use the word describe; that is not what an adjective does. It actually changes--modifies-- the image of the noun that we have in the mind. It does not describe it from a distance; it transforms the noun itself.

The Noun System

Complete the activity on p.25. Check your work using p.26. 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 6 - Lesson 9
lessons.body:
Verbs
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.30-34
 
➜ DISCUSS
Just like nouns, verbs can be singular or plural. The noun and verb must agree in number:
- The girl runs. (singular)
- The girls run. (plural)
• Think of other sentences like these.
• After reading the story together on p.34, discuss the story's point to focus on the action of the action verbs.
lessons.teacherNotes:
Nouns often make plurals by adding an s, but verbs often do the reverse.

If interested, look up the words in blue on p.34 in a dictionary, and discuss their meanings in this story.

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 6 - Lesson 10
lessons.body:
Linking Verbs
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.35-39 

➜ DISCUSS
Some linking verbs look like action verbs at first, but then you notice that they do not show action; they make equations. Can you find two words like that in the linking verb list on p.37? [smelled, looked] Compare how those words are used in the action verb list.

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 6 - Lesson 11
lessons.body:
Adverbs
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.40-41 

➜ PRACTICE
Can you figure out what each "adverb-teer" said to cast its spell on a bully? ["I will NOT submit;" "We are REALLY afraid;" "You stop RIGHT now."]

➜ DISCUSS
Just like with adjectives, you must use adverbs only when necessary, or they may make your writing too wordy and spoil the story.
lessons.teacherNotes: Stephen King once said, "The adverb is not your friend." Caution students against the wordy overuse of adverbs, and focus on the almost-always-damaging adverb "very."

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 6 - Lesson 12
lessons.body:
Practice
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town

➜ PRACTICE
p.42 
• Figure out what word each bold adverb modifies. It will always be a verb, an adjective, or another adverb. (no answer key available)  

➜ PRACTICE
p.43
• Follow the instructions. Note that, for now, not every word will get a label. Check your work using the answer key and review any difficult concepts.

lessons.body:
Conjunctions
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.44-45

➜ NOTE
We do NOT put a comma before the conjunction in a compound part of speech.
• Correct: Bob and Jane ran.
• Incorrect: Bob, and Jane ran.

➜ PRACTICE
p.46
lessons.teacherNotes: CO means together. The coordinating conjunctions CO-order what they compound.

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 6 - Lesson 14
lessons.body:
Conjunctions
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town

➜ PRACTICE
p.47
• Discuss the difference between what it means to coordinate and to subordinate.  

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.48

• Why do the mayor and Bartleby speak the way they do?

lessons.body:
Prepositions
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town
  
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.49-51 

➜ DISCUSS
Find five prepositions in the story on p.51. Read the words around them. Do you notice any patterns?
lessons.teacherNotes: The key is not to memorize a list of prepositions but to comprehend the FUNCTION of a preposition. There are many kinds, but they all show how two things are related.

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 6 - Lesson 16
lessons.body:
Prepositional Phrases
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.52-55

➜ NOTE
Prepositional phrases that modify nouns come immediately after the noun: dog on the dock

➜ PRACTICE
Find the prepositions in the story on p.54. Then write three sentences in which the prepositions act like adjectives and three in which they act like adverbs.

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 6 - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
Sentences
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.65-66 

➜ DISCUSS
Try reading only the predicate side of each sentence on p.66. Do they make sense by themselves? [No, without a subject, they are fragments.] 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.67-68
lessons.teacherNotes:
Here, grammar shows us that our thinking is binary. This is the meaning of CLARITY: to know what you are talking about and what you are saying about it.

If you know only the subject or the predicate, you have no idea what is being said. It takes two sides to make an idea.

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 6 - Lesson 26
lessons.body:
Subjects and Predicates
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.69-70

• Go through the flow path carefully and repeatedly as you read the explanations. Find everything that is described by pointing to the model.  

➜ PRACTICE
Follow the directions on p.71. Check using the Teacher Manual PDF. Remember: the D.O. must receive the action.  It must get verbed.
lessons.teacherNotes:
Logically, the D.O. comes first. In actual speaking sequence, the I.O. will be BETWEEN the AVP and the D.O.

Action verbs act, and linking verbs equate. 

∞ PDF Link: Grammar Town Teacher's Manual

Phrases
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town  
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.82-83

• Go slowly and carefully through these pages. Narrate often. Copy examples into your Grammar notebook.
lessons.teacherNotes: Do not fall prey to the stereotypes about how difficult the phrases are. The verbal phrases are learnable; there are only three kinds, and each one can be defined in one sentence. Students learn harder things than this every day in math. The verbals are critical to understanding the other three levels of grammar.

lessons.teacherNotes: APPOSE and JUXTAPOSE are synonyms; they both mean to put beside. JUXTAPOSE is more common.

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 6 - Lesson 36
lessons.body:
Verbal Phrases
⍞ Materials: Grammar Town 
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.86 

➜ PRACTICE
Write one sentence with a gerund phrase, one with a participle phrase, and one with an infinitive phrase.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITION
Phrase: a group of words that act like a SINGLE part of speech. The gerund phrase acts like a noun and as the subject of the verb.

A subject is a subject, and an object is an object. The object of anything will use object pronouns.

Be sure to pay attention to any added notes in the student dictation packet for the week. Only choose enough text so the student is learning 5-6 new words at a time.

terms.content:
Grammar: Grade 6
· Grammar Town: Is the word in brackets a n., pron., adj., v., adv., conj., prep., or interj.?

________ 1. The [train] pulled slowly into the station. 
________ 2. The baker [was] awake early this morning. 
________ 3. [The] sidewalk curved around the edge of the park. 
________ 4. A nice man sold papers [from] a little kiosk. 
________ 5. The wind blew [quietly] through the bell tower. 
________ 6. [Yes], the schools will be closed on Thursday. 
________ 7. The park opens at 7:00 [because] the holiday begins. 
________ 8. The shopkeeper gave [her] a cup of hot chocolate.

· Grammar Town: Name three coordinating conjunctions and three subordinating conjunctions. When do we use them?

· Grammar Town: Write two sentences that have a linking verb.

Dictation: Grade 6
· Dictate an unstudied passage with words similar in difficulty to what was dictated during the term.

Penmanship & Copywork: Grade 6
· Write 4 lines of poetry or a passage from memory in your best penmanship.

· Share your Copywork Journal with an adult.

Grammar: Grade 6
· Grammar Town: Is the word in brackets a subj., AVP, LVP, D.O., I.O., or S.C.? 

________ 9. The row of buildings [was] shady and lined with trees. 
________ 10. [We] could see the cinema from Front Street. 
________ 11. The teacher welcomed the new [students] to the class. 
________ 12. It is [good] to know different kinds of people. 
________ 13. The grocer gave [Susan] a sample of the cheese.

· Is the phrase in brackets prepositional, appositive, gerund, participial, or infinitive? 

________ 14. We loved [to run the motor]. 
________ 15. Mrs. Robertson, [the new principal], arrived at the school. 
________ 16. She ran [down the sidewalk], and we followed. 
________ 17. We enjoyed [catching fireflies]. 
________ 18. [Catching fireflies], Susie laughed and ran across the lawn.


· Grammar Town: Is the part in brackets a phrase, an independent clause, or a dependent clause? 

________ 19. Roberto chased the pigeons, and [Susie ran the other way]. 
________ 20. [Chasing the pigeons] was fun for the little kids. 
________ 21. Susie ran away [when Roberto chased the pigeons.] 


· Is the sentence declarative, imperative, interrogative, or exclamatory?
 
________ 22. When will the bus arrive at the new depot? 
________ 23. They are building an airport near Gramville Acres! 
________ 24. Take the cab over to the train station and wait for us. 
________ 25. The canal flows east beyond Grammar Town.

Dictation: Grade 6
· Dictate an unstudied passage with words similar in difficulty to what was dictated during the term.

Penmanship & Copywork: Grade 6
· Write 4 lines of poetry or a passage from memory in your best penmanship.

· Share your Copywork Journal with an adult.

Grammar: Grade 6
· Practice Town: Is the word in brackets a n., pron., adj., v., adv., conj., prep., or interj.?
 
________ 1. The [people] formed a line in front of the cinema. 
________ 2. The grocer [arranged] the fruit on the display. 
________ 3. [The] bus pulled slowly to a stop. 
________ 4. A chimney rose high above the top [of] the roof. 
________ 5. The children ran [quickly] up the sidewalk. 
________ 6. [Wow], it snowed two feet last night! 
________ 7. They did not arrive, [but] we kept waiting at the terminal. 
________ 8. [She] made the new mailman a chocolate cake. 

· Is the word in brackets a subj., AVP, LVP, D.O., I.O., or S.C.?
________ 9. The people in this neighborhood [are] very friendly. 
________ 10. [John] could see the new store from his attic window.
________ 11. The ice cream man gave Susan a [popsicle]. 
________ 12. It was [he] who rang the bell yesterday. 
________ 13. The baker gave [Roberto] a sack of hot rolls.

· Practice Town: Is the phrase in brackets prepositional, appositive, gerund, participial, or infinitive?

________ 14.[To think quickly and clearly] is a gift. 
________ 15. Robert Trotman, [the former mayor], was invited. 
________ 16. The ball sailed [over the roof] of the house. 
________ 17. [Giving good presents] is a real art. 
________ 18. [Fallen down completely], the old building looked sad. 

· Is the part in brackets a phrase, an independent clause, or a dependent clause? 
________ 19. Rochelle brought the dessert, and [we brought the soup]. 
________ 20. [Running for office] was an easy thing for Maria. 
________ 21. [As the pigeons flew over the square], we watched intently. 

· Is the sentence declarative, imperative, interrogative, or exclamatory? 
________ 22. Which store has the sweaters you told me about? 
________ 23. The bus almost hit the light pole at the corner! 
________ 24. Go to the market and buy some fresh vegetables and fish. 
________ 25. The Gram River flows down from the mountains.

Dictation: Grade 6
· Dictate an unstudied passage with words similar in difficulty to what was dictated during the term.

Penmanship & Copywork: Grade 6
· Write 4 lines of poetry or a passage from memory in your best penmanship.

· Share your Copywork Journal with an adult.
